text: Apollo 11 was the American spaceflight that first landed humans on the Moon.
Commander Neil Armstrong and lunar module pilot Buzz Aldrin landed the Apollo Lunar Module Eagle on July 20, 1969, at 20:17 UTC.
Armstrong became the first person to step onto the lunar surface six hours and 39 minutes later on July 21 at 02:56 UTC.
Aldrin joined Armstrong 19 minutes later.
Armstrong and Aldrin spent about two and a quarter hours together exploring the site Armstrong and Aldrin had named Tranquility Base upon landing.
Armstrong and Aldrin collected 47.5 pounds of lunar material to bring back to Earth.
Command module pilot Michael Collins flew the command module Columbia alone in lunar orbit while Armstrong and Aldrin were on the Moon's surface.
Armstrong and Aldrin spent 21 hours and 36 minutes on the lunar surface at a site Armstrong and Aldrin named Tranquility Base before lifting off to rejoin Columbia in lunar orbit.
Apollo 11 was launched by a Saturn V rocket from Kennedy Space Center on Merritt Island, Florida, on July 16 at 13:32 UTC.
Apollo 11 was the fifth crewed mission of NASA's Apollo program.

The Apollo spacecraft had three parts: a command module with a cabin for the three astronauts, the only part that returned to Earth; a service module, which supported the command module with propulsion, electrical power, oxygen, and water; and a lunar module that had two stages—a descent stage for landing on the Moon and an ascent stage to place the astronauts back into lunar orbit.

After being sent to the Moon by the Saturn V's third stage, the astronauts separated the spacecraft from the Saturn V's third stage and traveled for three days until the astronauts entered lunar orbit.
Armstrong and Aldrin then moved into Eagle and landed in the Sea of Tranquility on July 20.
The astronauts used Eagle's ascent stage to lift off from the lunar surface and rejoin Collins in the command module.
The astronauts jettisoned Eagle before the astronauts performed the maneuvers that propelled Columbia out of the last of Columbia's 30 lunar orbits onto a trajectory back to Earth.
The astronauts returned to Earth and splashed down in the Pacific Ocean on July 24 after more than eight days in space.
Armstrong's first step onto the lunar surface was broadcast on live television to a worldwide audience.
Armstrong described the event as 'one small step for a man, one giant leap for mankind.'
Apollo 11 effectively proved that landing humans on the Moon and returning them safely to Earth was possible, fulfilling President John F. Kennedy's goal of reaching the Moon by the end of the 1960s.
President Richard Nixon spoke with Armstrong and Aldrin via a telephone-radio transmission while Armstrong and Aldrin walked on the lunar surface.
Nixon congratulated Armstrong and Aldrin and said the call was 'the most historic telephone call ever made from the White House.'
A plaque left on the ladder of Eagle's descent stage read: 'Here men from the planet Earth first set foot upon the Moon, July 1969 A.D. We came in peace for all mankind.'
Nixon greeted the astronauts on the astronauts' return aboard the USS Hornet.
After the crew splashed down, the crew was quarantined for 21 days as a precaution against Moon germs.
The three astronauts Armstrong, Aldrin, and Collins were awarded the Presidential Medal of Freedom by President Nixon.
In 2009, the U.S. House of Representatives passed a resolution recognizing the 40th anniversary of the Apollo 11 flight and Apollo 11's crew members.
Neil Armstrong was born on August 5, 1930, in Wapakoneta, Ohio.
Armstrong was a naval aviator who flew combat missions during the Korean War before becoming a test pilot at the National Advisory Committee for Aeronautics High-Speed Flight Station.
Armstrong joined the NASA Astronaut Corps in 1962 and served as command pilot of Gemini 8 in 1966, becoming the first civilian to fly a U.S. spacecraft.
As spacecraft commander of Apollo 11, Armstrong earned the distinction of being the first person to walk on the Moon.
After the Apollo 11 mission, Armstrong resigned from NASA in 1971 and taught aerospace engineering at the University of Cincinnati until 1979.
Armstrong avoided publicity and rarely gave interviews about Apollo 11 in Armstrong's later years.
Armstrong died on August 25, 2012, at the age of 82, following complications from cardiovascular surgery.
Buzz Aldrin was born Edwin Eugene Aldrin Jr. on January 20, 1930, in Montclair, New Jersey.
Aldrin graduated from the United States Military Academy at West Point in 1951 and earned a Doctor of Science degree in astronautics from the Massachusetts Institute of Technology.
Aldrin's doctoral thesis on orbital rendezvous techniques contributed directly to NASA's docking procedures.
Aldrin was selected as part of NASA's third group of astronauts in 1963.
Aldrin performed the last mission of the Gemini program, Gemini 12, during which Aldrin spent over five hours on extravehicular activity.
As lunar module pilot on Apollo 11, Aldrin was the second person to walk on the Moon.
After the Apollo 11 mission, Aldrin became an advocate for human exploration of Mars.
Aldrin developed a concept called the Aldrin cycler, a spacecraft trajectory that would make regular trips between Earth and Mars.
Michael Collins was born on October 31, 1930, in Rome, Italy, where Collins's father was serving as a U.S. military attaché.
Collins graduated from West Point in 1952 and became an experimental test pilot at Edwards Air Force Base.
Collins was selected as part of NASA's third group of astronauts in 1963, along with Aldrin.
Collins flew on Gemini 10 in 1966, during which Collins performed two extravehicular activities.
On Apollo 11, Collins remained alone in lunar orbit aboard Columbia while Armstrong and Aldrin explored the surface below.
Collins later wrote that Collins felt no loneliness during the 48 minutes of each orbit when Collins was out of radio contact with Earth.
After the Apollo 11 mission, Collins left NASA and became the first director of the National Air and Space Museum in Washington, D.C.
Collins served in that role from 1971 to 1978, overseeing the National Air and Space Museum's opening in the museum's current building on the National Mall in 1976.
Collins died on April 28, 2021, at the age of 90, after a battle with cancer.
The Saturn V rocket that launched Apollo 11 was designated SA-506 and was the sixth Saturn V to fly.
The Saturn V stood 363 feet tall and weighed 6.5 million pounds when fully fueled.
The Saturn V's first stage, the S-IC, was powered by five F-1 engines that together produced 7.5 million pounds of thrust.
The F-1 engines burned a mixture of liquid oxygen and RP-1 kerosene for approximately 2 minutes and 41 seconds.
The second stage, the S-II, used five J-2 engines burning liquid hydrogen and liquid oxygen.
The S-II provided thrust for approximately 6 minutes, accelerating the spacecraft to nearly orbital velocity.
The third stage, the S-IVB, had a single J-2 engine that fired twice—once to achieve Earth orbit and again to send the spacecraft toward the Moon in a maneuver called translunar injection.
The launch took place at Launch Complex 39A at Kennedy Space Center, with an estimated one million spectators watching from the surrounding highways and beaches.
Vice President Spiro Agnew and former President Lyndon B. Johnson were among the dignitaries who attended the launch in person.
The Saturn V launch vehicle performed flawlessly, placing Apollo 11 into Apollo 11's translunar trajectory just 12 minutes after liftoff.
The lunar module Eagle was designed and built by Grumman Aircraft Engineering Corporation.
Eagle consisted of two stages: a descent stage with a throttleable engine for landing, and an ascent stage that served as the crew cabin and contained a fixed-thrust engine for returning to orbit.
Eagle's descent engine could be throttled from 10% to full thrust, allowing Armstrong to control the rate of descent during landing.
During the powered descent, program alarms sounded in the cockpit—the famous 1202 and 1201 alarms indicating the computer was being overloaded with tasks.
Flight controller Steve Bales in Mission Control determined the alarms were acceptable and called 'Go' for the landing to continue.
Armstrong took semi-automatic control of the lunar module in the final minutes of descent, steering Eagle past a boulder-strewn crater to find a safe landing spot.
When Eagle landed, only about 25 seconds of fuel remained in Eagle's descent stage.
Armstrong radioed 'Houston, Tranquility Base here. The Eagle has landed,' to which Charles Duke, the capsule communicator, replied, 'Roger, Tranquility, we copy you on the ground.'
The landing site was in the southern Sea of Tranquility, about 20 kilometers southwest of the crater Sabine D.
Armstrong described the lunar surface as covered with fine-grained material resembling powdered charcoal.

The astronauts deployed several scientific instruments on the lunar surface, including a passive seismic experiment to measure moonquakes and a laser ranging retroreflector that is still in use today.
Armstrong and Aldrin also deployed a solar wind composition experiment, which consisted of a sheet of aluminum foil exposed to the solar wind for approximately 77 minutes.
Armstrong and Aldrin planted an American flag on the lunar surface, though the flag fell over when Eagle's ascent stage lifted off.
The astronauts left behind medallions bearing the names of the three Apollo 1 astronauts who died in the 1967 launch pad fire, and two cosmonauts who were also deceased.
Upon the return to Earth, the Apollo 11 crew was placed in the Mobile Quarantine Facility, a modified Airstream trailer aboard the USS Hornet.
The quarantine lasted 21 days and was intended to prevent the potential spread of lunar pathogens to Earth's biosphere.
Scientists at the Lunar Receiving Laboratory in Houston examined the lunar samples and found no evidence of living organisms.
The quarantine requirement was dropped after Apollo 14 when the quarantine requirement became clear that the Moon was lifeless.
The lunar samples brought back by Apollo 11 were found to be basaltic rocks between 3.1 and 4.4 billion years old.

Analysis of the lunar samples fundamentally changed scientific understanding of the Moon's origin, supporting the giant-impact hypothesis that the Moon formed from debris after a Mars-sized body collided with Earth.
The Apollo 11 mission's success was broadcast to an estimated 600 million television viewers worldwide, making Apollo 11 one of the most watched events in human history.
The broadcast used a specially designed slow-scan television camera mounted on the equipment bay of Eagle's descent stage.
The slow-scan television camera was deployed by Armstrong as Armstrong descended the ladder, providing live footage of Armstrong's first steps.
The television signal was received by tracking stations in Australia—the Honeysuckle Creek station and the Parkes Radio Observatory—and relayed to Houston.
After completing the lunar surface activities, Armstrong and Aldrin returned to the lunar module and sealed the hatch.
Armstrong and Aldrin rested for several hours before beginning the launch preparations for Eagle's ascent stage.
The ascent engine fired successfully, lifting Eagle off the lunar surface and into orbit to rendezvous with Columbia.
Collins performed the docking maneuver, connecting Eagle to the command module Columbia's forward hatch.
After the crew transferred back to Columbia, Eagle was jettisoned and Eagle's orbit eventually decayed, causing Eagle to crash somewhere on the lunar surface.
Columbia's service propulsion system engine then fired to place the spacecraft on Columbia's return trajectory to Earth.
The return trip took approximately 60 hours.
Columbia re-entered Earth's atmosphere on July 24, 1969, at a speed of approximately 24,000 miles per hour.
The command module Columbia's heat shield protected the crew from temperatures exceeding 5,000 degrees Fahrenheit during re-entry.
Three parachutes deployed to slow Columbia's descent, and Columbia splashed down in the Pacific Ocean approximately 900 miles southwest of Hawaii at 16:50 UTC.
The Apollo 11 mission lasted a total of 8 days, 3 hours, 18 minutes, and 35 seconds from launch to splashdown.
Following the quarantine, the three astronauts Armstrong, Aldrin, and Collins embarked on a 38-day world tour called the 'Giant Leap' tour, visiting 24 countries.
Armstrong, Aldrin, and Collins were celebrated with ticker-tape parades in New York, Chicago, and Los Angeles.
The legacy of Apollo 11 extends far beyond the technical achievement; Apollo 11 demonstrated what humanity could accomplish through determination and international cooperation.
The Apollo 11 mission inspired generations of scientists, engineers, and explorers, and remains a defining moment of the 20th century.
The command module Columbia is on display at the National Air and Space Museum in Washington, D.C., where Columbia has been seen by millions of visitors since 1976.
